events.title: Doudna & Charpentier Publish Landmark CRISPR-Cas9 Paper
events.detail: Jennifer Doudna (UC Berkeley) and Emmanuelle Charpentier (Umeå University) publish 'A Programmable Dual-RNA–Guided DNA Endonuclease in Adaptive Bacterial Immunity' in Science on June 28, 2012. The paper demonstrates that Cas9 can be programmed with a single guide RNA to cut specific DNA sequences in vitro — the foundational proof-of-concept for CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing. This work earns the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.

events.title: Siksnys Lab Independently Demonstrates CRISPR Biochemistry
events.detail: Virginijus Šikšnys and colleagues at Vilnius University in Lithuania independently characterize the biochemical activity of Cas9. Their paper, submitted before Doudna/Charpentier's but published later, confirms the dual guide-RNA mechanism. Šikšnys shares the 2018 Kavli Prize in Nanoscience for this work, establishing that CRISPR's discovery was a shared scientific milestone.

sources.name: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (Gasiunas et al., 2012)

events.title: Zhang and Church Labs Edit Human Cells with CRISPR-Cas9
events.detail: Two papers published simultaneously in Science on January 3, 2013 demonstrate CRISPR-Cas9 editing in human and mouse cells for the first time. Feng Zhang (Broad Institute/MIT) and colleagues edit human and mouse cells (Science, DOI 10.1126/science.1231143). George Church's lab at Harvard (Cong et al. is Zhang; Church et al. is the second paper) also demonstrates multiplex genome editing in human stem cells. This leap from bacterial biochemistry to mammalian cells triggers the CRISPR biotech boom.

sources.name: Science, Cong et al. (Zhang Lab), Jan 2013
Science, Mali et al. (Church Lab), Jan 2013

events.title: CRISPR Used to Correct Hereditary Cataract Mutation in Mouse Embryos
events.detail: Researchers in China use CRISPR-Cas9 to correct a mutation causing hereditary cataracts in mouse embryos, demonstrating for the first time that CRISPR can repair a disease-causing gene in a whole organism. The work, published in Cell Research, marks the field's transition from proof-of-concept to disease-model research.

events.title: Editas Medicine Founded — First Dedicated CRISPR Biotech
events.detail: Editas Medicine is founded in November 2013 by Feng Zhang, Jennifer Doudna, George Church, David Liu, and Keith Joung — assembling the leading CRISPR scientists into a single commercial venture backed by $43M from Third Rock Ventures and other investors. Editas focuses on in vivo CRISPR editing for inherited eye diseases (CEP290/LCA10) and blood disorders. Doudna would later leave to found Caribou Biosciences and Intellia.

events.title: CRISPR Therapeutics Founded by Charpentier
events.detail: Emmanuelle Charpentier co-founds CRISPR Therapeutics in Zug, Switzerland in late 2013/early 2014 with CEO Rodger Novak and others. The company raises Series A funding and focuses on ex vivo cell engineering for blood disorders — a strategy that eventually produces Casgevy. Vertex Pharmaceuticals later partners with CRISPR Therapeutics in 2015 on what becomes the exa-cel (exagamglogene autotemcel) program.

events.title: Intellia Therapeutics Founded — In Vivo CRISPR Strategy
events.detail: Intellia Therapeutics is founded in April 2014 by Jennifer Doudna and colleagues, partnered with Caribou Biosciences (Doudna's other startup) and Atlas Venture. Intellia focuses on in vivo CRISPR delivery using lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) to edit cells inside the body, targeting the liver first. Its lead program NTLA-2001 targets transthyretin amyloidosis (ATTR) and becomes the first in vivo CRISPR therapy to demonstrate clinical proof-of-concept in 2021.

events.title: Scientists Call for Pause on Germline Editing at Napa Valley Summit
events.detail: On January 24, 2015, a confidential summit of 18 leading scientists and bioethicists meets in Napa Valley, California to discuss the implications of CRISPR for human germline editing. The group publishes a letter in Science calling for a voluntary moratorium on heritable human genome editing pending broader societal discussion — a direct parallel to the 1975 Asilomar Conference on recombinant DNA. The call is largely heeded internationally until the He Jiankui scandal in 2018.

events.title: First International Summit on Human Gene Editing — Washington DC
events.detail: December 1–3, 2015: The National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine co-hosts the First International Summit on Human Gene Editing in Washington DC with the Chinese Academy of Sciences and the Royal Society. The summit issues a statement that germline editing for reproduction would be 'irresponsible' at this time but that basic research, including on human embryos, should proceed under appropriate oversight. It also endorses somatic cell clinical research. The Summit sets a global governance framework that holds — until He Jiankui.

sources.name: National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine

events.title: World's First CRISPR Clinical Trial Launches in China
events.detail: On November 16, 2016, oncologist Lu You at Sichuan University's West China Hospital in Chengdu administers the first CRISPR-edited cells to a human patient — a lung cancer patient with a PD-1 gene disrupted in T cells to enhance anti-tumor immunity. The trial (NCT02793856) precedes the first US CRISPR trial by 2+ years, raising global debate about the pace of Chinese translational research and regulatory oversight. Nature News describes it as 'a new phase' in CRISPR's history.

events.title: USPTO Rules Broad Institute (Zhang) Owns Human-Cell CRISPR Patents
events.detail: On February 15, 2017, the US Patent and Trademark Office Patent Trial and Appeal Board rules that Feng Zhang and the Broad Institute do not interfere with UC Berkeley's earlier patent claims — meaning Broad's patents on CRISPR-Cas9 in eukaryotic (human) cells stand. The ruling triggers a years-long legal war between Broad/MIT and UC Berkeley over who controls the most commercially valuable CRISPR patents. Broad ultimately prevails in multiple US court decisions. The patent dispute affects billions of dollars in licensing revenue and shapes which companies license from which institution.

sources.name: USPTO Patent Trial and Appeal Board Decision (Feb 2017)

events.title: David Liu Lab Publishes Base Editing — C-to-T Edits Without DNA Breaks
events.detail: David Liu and colleagues at the Broad Institute publish 'Programmable editing of a target base in genomic DNA without double-stranded DNA cleavage' in Nature (October 2016 for first-generation base editors; third-generation BE3 published 2017). Base editors fuse a deaminase enzyme to a catalytically impaired Cas9 (nickase or dead Cas9) to chemically convert one DNA base to another (e.g., C to T or A to G) without creating double-strand breaks. This dramatically reduces off-target indel mutations and opens a new class of precision medicine. Liu's lab later develops adenine base editors (ABEs) and prime editors.

sources.name: Nature (Komor et al., 2016) & Nature (Gaudelli et al., 2017)

events.title: He Jiankui Announces CRISPR-Edited Babies — Global Outrage
events.detail: On November 26, 2018, Chinese scientist He Jiankui reveals at the Second International Summit on Human Genome Editing in Hong Kong that he used CRISPR to edit the CCR5 gene in human embryos — which were then implanted, resulting in the birth of twin girls (Lulu and Nana) and a third child in 2019. He claims the edit confers resistance to HIV. The announcement is immediately condemned by the global scientific community as premature, ethically reckless, and procedurally fraudulent. He circumvented informed consent procedures, falsified ethical approvals, and made edits whose off-target consequences are unknown. The experiment constitutes the first heritable human genome editing in history.

events.title: WHO Forms Expert Committee on Human Genome Editing Governance
events.detail: In January 2019, responding to He Jiankui's revelation, the World Health Organization establishes an Expert Advisory Committee on Developing Global Standards for Governance and Oversight of Human Genome Editing. The committee is chaired by Dr. Margaret Hamburg and eventually issues two landmark reports in 2021 recommending a global registry for human genome editing research, a 'whistle-blower' mechanism, and an international governance framework. The WHO also says it would be 'irresponsible' to proceed with clinical uses of germline editing at this time.

sources.name: WHO Expert Advisory Committee on Human Genome Editing (2019)

events.title: Victoria Gray Becomes First US Patient Treated with CRISPR for Sickle Cell
events.detail: In July 2019, Victoria Gray, a 34-year-old woman from Mississippi with severe sickle cell disease, becomes the first US patient to receive the CRISPR-based therapy CTX001 (later named Casgevy) as part of the CLIMB-SCD-121 Phase 3 trial. Her stem cells are extracted, edited to reactivate fetal hemoglobin production (by disrupting the BCL11A enhancer), and reinfused at the Sarah Cannon Research Institute in Nashville. Within months, Gray reports being crisis-free for the first time in her adult life. Her story, documented by NPR journalist Rob Stein over multiple years, becomes the defining human narrative of CRISPR gene therapy.

events.title: He Jiankui Sentenced to 3 Years in Prison by Chinese Court
events.detail: On December 30, 2019, a Chinese court in Shenzhen sentences He Jiankui to three years in prison and fines him three million yuan (~$430,000 USD) for 'illegal medical practice.' Two colleagues receive lesser sentences. The verdict confirms that He acted without proper institutional approval, fabricated ethical reviews, and acted for 'personal fame and gain.' The speed of the verdict — and its framing as a criminal matter rather than scientific misconduct — reflects China's desire to assert regulatory control over CRISPR research. He is released in 2022.

events.title: Nobel Prize in Chemistry: Doudna and Charpentier Awarded for CRISPR
events.detail: On October 7, 2020, the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences awards the Nobel Prize in Chemistry to Jennifer Doudna (UC Berkeley) and Emmanuelle Charpentier (Max Planck Unit for the Science of Pathogens, Berlin) 'for the development of a method for genome editing.' It is the first Nobel Prize awarded to an all-female pair, and the first for a technology with direct clinical applications since Montagnier and Barré-Sinoussi's 1984 HIV work. Feng Zhang, widely seen as equally deserving, is notably absent — a reflection of the ongoing patent and priority dispute. The Nobel Committee explicitly cites the technology's 'infinite possibilities' for treating inherited diseases and cancers.

events.title: Prime Editing Introduced — 'Search-and-Replace' Precision Genome Editing
events.detail: David Liu's laboratory at the Broad Institute publishes 'Search-and-replace genome editing without double-strand breaks or donor DNA' in Nature on October 21, 2019. Prime editing uses a modified Cas9 (H840A nickase) fused to a reverse transcriptase, guided by a prime editing guide RNA (pegRNA), to write new genetic information directly into a specified genomic site. Unlike base editing (limited to single-base transitions), prime editing can install all 12 types of point mutations and small insertions/deletions, covering ~89% of known pathogenic variants. The technology is hailed as the most versatile precision editing method to date.

events.title: Intellia NTLA-2001 Phase 1: First In Vivo CRISPR Therapy Shows Clinical Proof-of-Concept
events.detail: Published in the New England Journal of Medicine on June 26, 2021, Intellia Therapeutics' NTLA-2001 Phase 1 trial data demonstrate that a single infusion of CRISPR components in lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) can permanently edit the TTR gene in liver cells in vivo — eliminating production of disease-causing misfolded transthyretin protein in patients with hereditary ATTR amyloidosis. Patients in the high-dose cohort show up to 87% reduction in serum TTR levels, sustained through 12 months. This is the first clinical demonstration of in vivo CRISPR editing in humans, proving LNP delivery can achieve therapeutic CRISPR editing without ex vivo cell manipulation.

sources.name: New England Journal of Medicine (Gillmore et al., Jun 26, 2021)

events.title: Phase 3 CLIMB-SCD-121: Exa-cel Achieves 93.5% Freedom from SCD Crises
events.detail: Data from the CLIMB-SCD-121 Phase 3 trial of exagamglogene autotemcel (exa-cel, CTX001) for sickle cell disease, published in December 2021 and updated in 2023, show that 93.5% of treated patients (29 of 31 evaluable) remain free of severe vaso-occlusive crises (VOC) for at least 12 months. All 29 evaluable patients achieve hemoglobin levels above 11 g/dL. The therapy works by reactivating fetal hemoglobin (HbF) via CRISPR editing of the BCL11A erythroid enhancer in the patient's own hematopoietic stem cells — a one-time, potentially curative treatment.

sources.name: New England Journal of Medicine (Frangoul et al., 2021 and Locatelli et al., 2022)

events.title: EDIT-101 Phase 1/2: Editas Reports Vision Improvement in LCA10 Patients
events.detail: Editas Medicine and Allergan report interim Phase 1/2 results from the BRILLIANCE trial of EDIT-101, an in vivo CRISPR therapy for CEP290-associated Leber congenital amaurosis type 10 (LCA10). The therapy is delivered directly into the subretinal space via adeno-associated virus (AAV5). Of the 14 patients dosed, 6 show clinically meaningful improvements in visual acuity and light sensitivity. This represents the first direct in vivo CRISPR editing in the eye and establishes proof-of-concept for ocular gene editing. Editas later pauses BRILLIANCE to refocus pipeline strategy.

sources.name: Nature Medicine (Maeder et al., 2019 — preclinical; clinical results via NEJM Evidence 2023)

events.title: Victoria Gray: 4+ Years Sickle Cell-Free — NEJM Follow-Up Published
events.detail: In December 2022, an updated follow-up report in the New England Journal of Medicine documents that Victoria Gray and other early sickle cell exa-cel recipients remain in clinical remission more than four years after treatment. Gray reports living a normal life — working, exercising, and raising her children without sickle cell crises. Her case becomes central to FDA and congressional discussions on CRISPR therapy access and the definition of 'functional cure.' The durability of HbF reactivation confirms the mechanistic soundness of the BCL11A enhancer editing approach.

sources.name: New England Journal of Medicine (Frangoul et al., 2022)

events.title: FDA Advisory Committee Votes 16-0 for Casgevy Approval
events.detail: On October 31, 2023, the FDA's Cellular, Tissue and Gene Therapies Advisory Committee (CTGTAC) votes unanimously 16-0 to recommend approval of exagamglogene autotemcel (exa-cel, brand name Casgevy) for sickle cell disease. The following day, the committee votes 15-0 for approval for transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia (TDT). Committee members praise the extraordinary efficacy and durability data from the CLIMB trials. Key concerns raised include the long-term durability beyond 5 years and the need for post-market surveillance studies on off-target editing effects.

sources.name: FDA Cellular, Tissue and Gene Therapies Advisory Committee Meeting (Oct 31 – Nov 1, 2023)

events.title: UK MHRA Approves Casgevy — World's First CRISPR Therapy Approval
events.detail: On November 16, 2023, the UK Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) becomes the first regulatory body in the world to grant marketing authorization for a CRISPR-based gene therapy — approving Casgevy (exagamglogene autotemcel) for sickle cell disease and transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia in patients aged 12 and older. The UK approval precedes the US FDA by three weeks, marking a major milestone in British life sciences post-Brexit. The MHRA's faster timeline reflects its new post-Brexit regulatory independence.

events.title: FDA Approves Casgevy for Sickle Cell Disease — Historic US CRISPR First
events.detail: On December 8, 2023, the US Food and Drug Administration approves Casgevy (exagamglogene autotemcel, exa-cel) — developed by CRISPR Therapeutics and Vertex Pharmaceuticals — for the treatment of sickle cell disease in patients aged 12 and older. Casgevy becomes the first CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing therapy approved in the United States. The therapy is priced at $2.2 million per one-time treatment. On the same day, the FDA approves Lyfgenia (lovotibeglogene autotemcel, lovo-cel) by bluebird bio — a separate lentiviral gene therapy (not CRISPR) — for the same indication. The dual approval marks a historic day for gene therapy.

events.title: FDA Approves Casgevy for Beta-Thalassemia (TDT) — Second CRISPR Indication
events.detail: On January 16, 2024, the FDA approves Casgevy for transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia (TDT) in patients aged 12 and older — the second approved indication for a CRISPR-based therapy. The CLIMB-THAL-111 Phase 3 trial showed that 89% of 52 evaluable patients achieved transfusion independence for at least 12 consecutive months. The therapy addresses a disease where patients require regular blood transfusions throughout their lives. The approval expands Casgevy's market and demonstrates the versatility of the BCL11A editing approach for hemoglobin disorders.

events.title: European Medicines Agency Conditionally Approves Casgevy
events.detail: In February 2024, the European Medicines Agency (EMA) grants conditional marketing authorization to Casgevy (exagamglogene autotemcel) for both sickle cell disease and transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia. The conditional approval follows the CHMP (Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use) positive opinion issued in December 2023. It requires Vertex and CRISPR Therapeutics to provide additional long-term follow-up data and post-market surveillance. The EMA approval opens the EU market for the first CRISPR therapy, though access and reimbursement timelines vary by member state.

events.title: First Commercial Casgevy Infusions Begin at Authorized US Treatment Centers
events.detail: Throughout 2024, Vertex Pharmaceuticals and CRISPR Therapeutics activate authorized treatment centers (ATCs) across the United States to administer commercial Casgevy infusions. The process is arduous: patients undergo stem cell mobilization, apheresis, conditioning chemotherapy, and then infusion of the edited product — a process taking 6–12 months per patient. The Sarah Cannon Research Institute in Nashville (where Victoria Gray was treated in trials) is among the early ATCs. Vertex reports manufacturing and treatment center scale-up as the primary near-term constraints on commercial uptake.

events.title: Intellia Initiates Phase 3 Trial for In Vivo CRISPR ATTR Amyloidosis (NTLA-2001)
events.detail: Building on Phase 1 data showing up to 93% reduction in TTR protein with a single CRISPR infusion, Intellia Therapeutics initiates its Phase 3 MAGNITUDE trial for NTLA-2001 in 2024 for hereditary ATTR amyloidosis with polyneuropathy. The Phase 3 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled design compares NTLA-2001 to placebo on the Neuropathy Impairment Score. If successful, NTLA-2001 would become the first in vivo CRISPR gene therapy to receive FDA approval, potentially disrupting the existing $3B+ RNAi and small molecule ATTR market.

events.title: Beam Therapeutics Reports Phase 1 Base Editing Safety Data for Sickle Cell
events.detail: Beam Therapeutics, founded by David Liu and colleagues, reports initial Phase 1 data from its BEACON-101 study of BEAM-101 — an adenine base editing (ABE) therapy for sickle cell disease. BEAM-101 uses base editors to reactivate fetal hemoglobin without creating double-strand DNA breaks, potentially improving the safety profile over CRISPR-Cas9 nuclease approaches. Early data indicate successful HbF reactivation and acceptable safety in initial patients. Base editing represents the next generation of CRISPR-derived therapies entering clinical translation.

events.title: Prime Medicine Enters Clinical Stage with Prime Editing Therapy
events.detail: Prime Medicine, founded by David Liu and Bayer AG, advances its lead prime editing program PM359 (for chronic granulomatous disease, CGD) into clinical trials in 2025, representing one of the first clinical tests of prime editing technology in humans. Prime editing allows precise installation of any desired edit — including all 12 point mutation types and small insertions/deletions — without double-strand breaks. The milestone marks the third generation of CRISPR-derived gene editing technology entering clinical development, following classic CRISPR-Cas9 and base editing.

sources.name: Prime Medicine Pipeline and Corporate Updates 2025

events.title: Allogeneic CRISPR-Edited CAR-T Cells Advance in Cancer Immunotherapy Trials
events.detail: Multiple companies — including CRISPR Therapeutics (CTX110, CTX120, CTX130), Caribou Biosciences, and Precision BioSciences — advance allogeneic (off-the-shelf) CRISPR-edited CAR-T cell therapies in Phase 1/2 trials for blood cancers including B-cell lymphoma, multiple myeloma, and T-cell lymphoma. CRISPR editing allows deletion of T-cell receptor genes (preventing graft-versus-host disease) and PD-1 (immune checkpoint), enabling more potent and persistent anti-tumor responses. Phase 1 data from CTX110 show objective response rates in heavily pre-treated patients. If successful, these would represent off-the-shelf alternatives to personalized autologous CAR-T therapies.
